content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 15, 2018

Shannon Eckmeyer
Assistant City Attorney
City Attorney's Office
555 Santa Clara Street
Vallejo, CA 94590

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-076(a)

Dear Ms. Eckmeyer:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vallejo City Councilmember
Robert McConnell regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should
contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

1. May Councilmember McConnell participate in the Accessory Dwelling Unit ("ADU")
ordinance decision where he owns five properties, four residential and one commercial, that have
the potential for an ADU?

2. Does the public generally exception apply?

CONCLUSION

No, he may not participate. Under the Act, he has a reasonably foreseeable and material
financial interest in the ADU ordinance decision. The public generally exception is not applicable
because the impact of this decision on his financial interest will be unique, as compared to the
general public.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-076(a)
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR

You are an Assistant City Attorney for the City of Vallejo ("City"). The Vallejo City
Council recently deliberated on amendments to the ADU zoning code ordinance requirements for
ADUs.² At the time of this request, the ordinance was set as an action item for a second reading and
final approval for the City Council on September 25, 2018. It is possible that this matter would be
reopened in the public process at that time, for further deliberations and decisions. The amendments
do not add or delete any zoning districts where ADUs might be built, but clarify various zoning
issues. The zoning ordinance as amended requires that an ADU lot must be zoned for residential
use, and with only one primary unit to be eligible for an ADU. The amendments also address height
limits, floor area limit, side and rear yard setbacks, access requirements, parking requirements.
Most significantly, the amendments delete the previous requirement that the property owner live in
one of the units to be eligible for an ADU on the parcel.

Councilmember McConnell owns four residential properties in the City that may be eligible
for the construction of an ADU under the proposed zoning ordinance.³ These four residential
properties are in zoning districts that allow ADUs and have sufficient space to build an ADU under
the present ordinance and proposed amendments. With the proposed amendment to the owner
occupancy requirement, Councilmember McConnell will be able construct an ADU on all four
properties instead of being limited to one. In accordance with an earlier advice letter on this matter,
Councilmember McConnell has recused himself from participation in this decision.⁴

You now request further advice on whether the public generally exception applies to
Councilmember McConnell and provide additional facts to make this determination. The City has a
population of approximately 120,000. Per the 2014 American Community Survey of the U.S.
Census Bureau, there are 41,802 occupied residential units in the City, and 48.2 percent of those
units are rentals. From that data, you conclude that roughly half of the residential homes in the City
are owned by the occupant. As to the real property parcels impacted by the ADU ordinance
amendments, you state that the City has 29,282 residentially zoned parcels. Of that number, an
estimated 24,432 parcels are zoned for low density residential and/or medium density residential
and would meet the proposed lot size requirement of greater than or equal to 4,500 square
feet. These 24,432 parcels (residentially zoned property) could qualify for ADUs.

² Under the City ordinance, "Accessory Dwelling Unit" means a residential dwelling unit attached to or
detached from the primary single-family residential dwelling unit on the same property which provides complete
independent living facilities for one or more persons. It shall include permanent provisions for living, sleeping, eating,
cooking, and sanitation.

³ Councilmember McConnell also owns a commercial property with sufficient physical space to construct an
ADU, but it is zoned as Linear Commercial and does not allow residential use. Therefore, under the present ordinance
and proposed amendments, he would not be permitted to construct an ADU there. All of Councilmember McConnell's
remaining residential properties (other than the four referenced above), while properly zoned for an ADU, physically
have insufficient space to add an ADU and are not at issue here.

⁴ Mooney Advice Letter, No. A-18-076.

File No. A-18-076(a)
Page No. 3

On behalf of Councilmember McConnell, you would like to know if he has a conflict of
interest in participating in the ADU decision and specifically whether the public generally
exception applies to him with respect to ADU decision.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. As applicable to the
facts provided, Section 87103 identifies the following applicable interests:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received
by, the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

For the decision at issue, Councilmember McConnell has an interest in his rental business as
a business entity and source of income, the tenants of his rentals as sources of income, and in the
four residential parcels and one commercial parcel as real property.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed reasonably foreseeable if
the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is
the "subject" of a proceeding, and deemed materially affected, when the decision determines the
parcel's zoning or rezoning (other than a zoning decision applicable to all properties designated in
that category). (Regulation 18701 and18702.2(a)(2).)

Councilmember McConnell's four real property residential parcels are the subject of this
zoning ordinance amendment decision. With the removal of the owner occupancy requirement, the
amendments will allow ADUs on all four of his properties. Therefore, under Regulations 18701 and
18702.2(a)(2), the effect of decision relating to the proposed zoning ordinance on the
Councilmember's interests in each of those properties is presumed reasonably foreseeable, and
material.⁵ He may not participate in this decision due to his conflict of interest under the Act.

_____________
⁵ We do not further analyze the effect of the proposed zoning ordinance on the Councilmember's business
interests or source of income, including tenants as a source of income, because we have determined that the
Councilmember is disqualified from participating based on his real property interests.

File No. A-18-076(a)
Page No. 4

Public Generally Exception

Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Known
as the "public generally exception," an official may participate despite having a financial interest
where the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on
his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment.
(Regulation 18703(a).)

For purposes of the exception, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25
percent of (1) the businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, or
residential real property; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation
18703(b).) A "unique effect" includes a disproportionate effect on the official's financial interests.
(Regulation 18703(c).) Specifically, a unique effect includes a disproportionate effect on:

(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from the
cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest.

(Regulation 18703(c)(3).)

Your facts indicate that 24,432 of the 29,282 residential real property parcels, will be
impacted by the ADU ordinance amendments. This is approximately 83 percent of all residential
real properties in the City, and therefore is a significant segment. However, Councilmember
McConnell owns four residential parcels that will be impacted by the decision. The ADU ordinance
amendments will allow increased use, income and development potential on all four of his parcels.
Absent facts to the contrary, it appears that this is a substantially greater effect than an average
homeowner will experience. He will be uniquely affected by the decision as compared to the
significant segment of the public by the cumulative effect of the decision on his real property
interests. Therefore, the public generally exception does not apply and Councilmember McConnell
will have a conflict of interest should he participate in the ADU decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

•

(Section 87103(b).)
